Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge, Sixth Edition — phase gate, page 556

Core Meaning: A phase review is a formal assessment at the end of a project phase used to evaluate results and support a continuation decision.
Expansion: A phase review examines whether phase deliverables, performance, risks, and readiness satisfy the criteria for moving forward. It is used to support decisions such as continue, rework, hold, or terminate. In PMI-aligned practice, it serves as a governance checkpoint between major portions of work.
Contrast: Phase gate — a phase review is the evaluation activity; a phase gate is the control point or decision mechanism associated with moving between phases.
Example: After completing design, a product team holds a phase review to confirm scope maturity, budget status, and technical readiness before authorising build work.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A project management office, a structure that standardizes project-related governance processes and facilitates the sharing of resources, methodologies, tools, and techniques.
Expansion: A PMO supports consistency and governance across projects, programs, or portfolios. Its role can range from supportive to controlling, depending on the organization. It often provides standards, reporting, coaching, and oversight to improve delivery performance.
Contrast: Project manager — a PMO provides organizational support and governance; a project manager leads a specific project.
Example: An enterprise PMO publishes templates, reviews project status reports, and helps teams apply common scheduling and risk practices.
Source: PMI Knowledgebase — PMO. See Project management office, page 153

Core Meaning: An intentional action to ensure the future performance of the project work is aligned with the project management plan.
Expansion: Preventive action is taken to reduce the probability of a potential negative effect before it occurs. It is different from corrective action because it addresses anticipated issues rather than existing variances. PMI treats it as part of project control and quality management, where proactive adjustments help keep work aligned with the plan.
Contrast: Corrective action — preventive action avoids a future problem; corrective action fixes an existing deviation.
Example: A project team adds an extra design review before release to prevent defects from reaching production.
Source: PMBOK Guide, Preventive action, p. 89

Core Meaning: A process-based project management methodology (PRojects IN Controlled Environments) used widely in the UK and internationally.
Expansion: PRINCE2 is a structured, process-driven method organized around defined principles, themes, and processes, with strong emphasis on continued business justification, clearly defined roles, and managing by stages and by exception. It is agnostic about technical delivery and is often adopted as an organizational standard alongside or instead of PMI-based approaches. The PMP exam is not based on PRINCE2, but practitioners may encounter it in governance-heavy environments.
Contrast: PMBOK Guide — PRINCE2 is a prescriptive methodology with set processes; the PMBOK Guide is a principles-and-domains body of knowledge, not a methodology.
Example: A UK government programme mandates PRINCE2 stage boundaries and a project board for governance decisions.
Source: Not a formal PMI glossary entry; widely used industry methodology (AXELOS).

Core Meaning: A structure that supports program governance, integration, oversight, and administrative coordination for a program.
Expansion: A program management office is the support and integration function established for a program to help coordinate its components and sustain effective governance. In PMI usage, it is distinct from a project management office because it is oriented to program-level integration, benefits, reporting, decision support, and cross-component coordination. It is foundational because many programs rely on this structure to maintain alignment, visibility, and control across multiple related efforts.
Deep Dive:
What it is: A program management office, often abbreviated PgMO, is an enabling structure that provides coordination, reporting, governance support, and management services for a program. It helps the program manager and component leaders operate as a coherent system.

Why it matters and where it applies: programs typically span multiple projects, stakeholders, dependencies, and benefit streams. A program management office creates consistency in planning, reporting, risk visibility, change coordination, and decision support, which is especially valuable in enterprise change programs and multi-vendor or multi-workstream initiatives.

The most common misconception: that a program management office is simply the same thing as a PMO at a larger scale. In practice, a PMO may support many projects across an organization, while a program management office is focused on one program and on integrating its components to realize benefits.

How it shows up in real projects: in a merger integration program, the program management office may consolidate status from workstreams, manage dependency logs, coordinate governance meetings, maintain issue escalation paths, and support benefits tracking for leadership.
Contrast: Project management office — a program management office supports a specific program and its integrated benefits delivery; a project management office typically provides broader project support, standards, or oversight across projects.
Example: A retailer's omnichannel transformation program uses a program management office to coordinate release timing, executive reporting, dependency management, and governance across e-commerce, store systems, and supply-chain projects.
Source: The Standard for Program Management, Fifth Edition — Program management office, page 133

Core Meaning: A structured examination of a project's processes, controls, compliance, and performance to assess whether it is being managed effectively and according to required standards or expectations.
Expansion: A project audit is a formal review that evaluates selected aspects of a project such as governance, financial control, quality practices, risk management, procurement compliance, or adherence to organisational procedures. Unlike routine status monitoring, it is typically independent or semi-independent and is intended to provide assurance, identify gaps, and recommend corrective action or lessons for future work. It is commonly used in regulated, high-risk, or high-visibility projects where objective oversight is needed beyond normal project reporting.
Contrast: Project review — a project review assesses status, progress, or readiness; a project audit is a more formal assurance-focused examination of controls, compliance, and management effectiveness.
Example: During a public-sector IT modernization effort, an internal audit team reviews procurement records, change approvals, risk logs, and reporting controls to confirm the project is complying with governance requirements.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A concise early description of a proposed project used to summarize purpose, scope, context, and key expectations before fuller authorization or planning.
Expansion: Project brief is a commonly used practitioner term for a short document that outlines the project's rationale, objectives, major deliverables, constraints, and stakeholders at a high level. PMI materials use nearby formal concepts such as business case and project charter more consistently than project brief as a standard glossary term. In practice, a project brief often serves as a precursor or lightweight input to chartering, funding, or initiation decisions.
Contrast: Project charter — a project brief is often an informal or preliminary summary; a project charter formally authorizes the project and the project manager's use of organizational resources.
Example: Before funding a customer-portal redesign, a product sponsor circulates a two-page project brief describing the business problem, target users, expected outcomes, budget range, and initial timeline assumptions.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A document issued by the project sponsor that formally authorizes the existence of a project and gives the project manager authority to apply organizational resources.
Expansion: The project charter is the formal starting point that authorizes the project and identifies its purpose, high-level objectives, and initial authority. It typically names the project manager and establishes the sponsor's support for the work. PMI treats it as a key governance document that connects the project to business need and organizational commitment.
Deep Dive:
What it is: A project charter is the document that officially starts a project. It gives the project manager authority and records the high-level reason, objectives, and boundaries for the work.

Why it matters and where it applies: it matters because no project should begin without clear authorization and alignment to business need. It is used in predictive, agile, and hybrid environments as the formal basis for initiating work and clarifying accountability.

The most common misconception: that the charter is a detailed plan. It is not a full schedule or requirements document; it is a high-level authorization and direction document.

How it shows up in real projects: a sponsor signs the charter for a system replacement project, names the project manager, and approves the initial budget and high-level scope so planning can begin.
Contrast: Business case — a business case explains why the work should be done; a project charter formally authorizes the project to begin.
Example: A healthcare organization issues a charter for an electronic records upgrade, naming the sponsor, project manager, objectives, and initial authority to use resources.
Source: PMI Lexicon of Project Management Terms — project charter

Core Meaning: Project closure is the process of formally completing the project or phase and transitioning, handing off, or ending the work in an orderly way.
Expansion: In PMI practice, project closure confirms that planned work is complete or that the project is being terminated appropriately, and that acceptance, records, lessons learned, and final transitions are handled. It includes administrative, contractual, financial, and knowledge-capture activities needed to close out the effort responsibly. Closure turns completed delivery into an accountable organisational outcome rather than simply stopping work.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Project closure is the formal set of actions that ends a project or phase:
- Verifies completion criteria
- Secures acceptance where required
- Closes procurements and accounts
- Archives records
- Transfers ownership of deliverables or ongoing responsibilities

Why it matters and where it applies: without closure, organisations lose lessons, leave obligations unresolved, and create confusion about support, ownership, or remaining risk. It applies to predictive, agile, and hybrid work alike, though the exact artifacts differ by life cycle and governance model.

The most common misconception: that closure is just a final meeting or a declaration that the team is done. In practice it is a governed transition activity that protects value, confirms accountability, and preserves organisational knowledge.

How it shows up in real projects: after deploying a new customer portal, the team obtains business sign-off, transitions support to operations, finalises vendor invoices, captures lessons learned, releases resources, and archives project records.
Contrast: Phase closure — project closure ends the entire project, while phase closure formally completes only one phase before the next begins or before a phase-gate decision.
Example: A hospital equipment-upgrade project closes by obtaining final acceptance from clinical leadership, transferring maintenance documents to operations, reconciling supplier payments, and recording lessons learned for future sites.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide) Sixth Edition, Close Project or Phase, Section 4.7; also reflected in Process Groups: A Practice Guide

Core Meaning: Documents that are not part of the project management plan but are used to manage the project.
Expansion: Project documents are the working records and supporting artifacts used throughout the project. They include items such as logs, registers, schedules, assumptions, and issue records. These documents are updated as the project progresses and support planning, execution, monitoring, and control. PMI distinguishes them from the project management plan because they support the work rather than define the approved management approach.
Contrast: Project management plan — the project management plan defines the approved approach for managing the project; project documents are the supporting records used to run it.
Example: A team maintains an issue log, risk register, stakeholder register, and assumption log as project documents during delivery.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Seventh Edition, project documents, page 244

Core Meaning: A framework, functions, and processes that guide project management activities in order to meet organizational or strategic goals.
Expansion: Project governance defines how direction, oversight, decision-making, and accountability are structured for a project. In PMI usage, it connects project work to organizational governance and strategy by establishing authorities, policies, escalation paths, and control mechanisms. It helps ensure that project decisions are made consistently, transparently, and in alignment with intended benefits and risk tolerances.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Project governance is the system through which a project is directed and overseen. It includes decision rights, review structures, roles, policies, and criteria used to steer the project and judge performance.

Why it matters and where it applies: projects operate within larger organizational systems, so they need clear authority and oversight to stay aligned with strategy, funding constraints, compliance expectations, and stakeholder priorities. Governance is especially important in complex, regulated, cross-functional, or high-visibility initiatives where unclear authority can slow decisions or increase risk.

The most common misconception: that project governance is just status reporting or executive approval gates. In practice, reporting is only one visible output; governance is the broader structure that clarifies who decides what, how trade-offs are made, when escalation occurs, and how alignment is maintained.

How it shows up in real projects:
- Steering committees
- Sponsor decision forums
- Phase-gate reviews
- Delegated authorities
- Escalation thresholds
- Change approval rules
- Clear accountability for benefits, risk, and value delivery
Contrast: Governance framework — project governance applies that broader governing structure specifically to a project; a governance framework may exist at organizational, portfolio, program, or project level.
Example: In an enterprise ERP rollout, the sponsor approves scope trade-offs above a set threshold, a steering committee reviews benefit realization monthly, and the project manager escalates schedule risks through defined governance channels.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide) Seventh Edition and The Standard for Project Management, page 237

Core Meaning: The early effort to define and authorize a new project or phase, typically culminating in an approved charter.
Expansion: Project initiation defines and authorizes a new project or a new phase, usually producing an approved project charter and an initial identification of stakeholders. It establishes the project's purpose, high-level scope, and the project manager's authority. Successful initiation aligns the project with organizational strategy and business need before significant resources are committed.
Contrast: Project planning - initiation authorizes and frames the project, while planning works out the detailed how, when, and how much.
Example: A sponsor signs a charter authorizing a warehouse-automation project and names the project manager, completing initiation.
Source: PMBOK Guide - Initiating Process Group

Core Meaning: The knowledge area concerned with identifying, defining, combining, unifying, and coordinating the various processes and project management activities so the project works as a coherent whole.
Expansion: Project Integration Management brings the many parts of a project together so decisions made in one area remain aligned with the project's overall objectives, constraints, and governance. In PMI's process-based framing, it includes developing foundational documents, directing work, managing knowledge, monitoring and controlling the project holistically, performing integrated change control, and closing the project or phase. It matters because projects fail less often from a single isolated mistake than from disconnected decisions, unresolved trade-offs, and weak coordination across planning, execution, and control. The concept is central to the project manager's role because it links strategy, delivery, stakeholder expectations, and change into one managed system.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Project Integration Management is the discipline of making the project function as one system rather than a collection of separate plans and specialist activities. It connects the charter, plans, work, changes, knowledge, and closure decisions into a unified management approach.

Why it matters and where it applies: every project contains interdependencies across scope, schedule, cost, quality, resources, risk, procurement, and stakeholders. Integration matters wherever one decision affects several others, which means it applies on virtually all projects, whether predictive, adaptive, or hybrid.

The most common misconception: that integration is just administration or document consolidation. In practice, it is the active leadership work of balancing trade-offs, resolving conflicts, sequencing decisions, and ensuring that changes support the project's intended outcomes.

How it shows up in real projects: a change request that improves product scope may also affect delivery timing, funding, risk exposure, vendor commitments, and stakeholder communications. Integration management is the mechanism for evaluating that full impact and deciding coherently rather than in silos.
Contrast: Project coordination — coordination is an activity within delivery; Project Integration Management is the broader discipline of unifying decisions, processes, and change across the whole project.
Example: On an ERP implementation, the project manager assesses a requested reporting enhancement by examining its impact on scope, testing effort, training, deployment date, vendor work, and budget before approving it through integrated change control.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide) Sixth Edition, Project Integration Management, Chapter 4, pages 69-113

Core Meaning: The practice of using existing knowledge and creating new knowledge to achieve project objectives and contribute to organizational learning.
Expansion: Project knowledge management covers both explicit knowledge that can be codified and tacit knowledge held in experience and relationships. It relies on knowledge-sharing methods such as communities of practice, work shadowing, and discussions, alongside documentation tools. The aim is to prevent loss of know-how when people leave and to reuse insight across the project and organization. PMI frames the related effort as managing project knowledge throughout execution.
Contrast: Lessons learned — lessons learned capture retrospective insight at points in time, whereas project knowledge management is the ongoing flow and reuse of knowledge.
Example: The team runs regular knowledge-sharing sessions and maintains a register so tacit insight is captured before specialists roll off.
Source: PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition (Manage Project Knowledge)

Core Meaning: The collection of tools and techniques used to gather, integrate, and disseminate the outputs of project management processes.
Expansion: A PMIS is part of the organization's enterprise environmental factors and provides automated tools such as scheduling software, work authorization systems, configuration management systems, and information repositories. It supports planning, executing, and monitoring by giving the team access to schedules, budgets, and performance data. It is used throughout the project to collect and distribute information.
Contrast: Work performance information - the PMIS is the system, while work performance information is the analyzed data that flows through it.
Example: A team uses MS Project plus a shared dashboard and document repository as its PMIS to track schedule and cost status.
Source: PMBOK Guide - project management information system

Core Meaning: An organizational structure that standardizes the project-related governance processes and facilitates the sharing of resources, methodologies, tools, and techniques.
Expansion: A project management office, or PMO, provides centralized support and governance for projects and sometimes for programs and portfolios as well. Its role can be supportive, controlling, or directive depending on how much authority the organization assigns to it. In PMI practice, it commonly maintains standards, coordinates reporting, enables capability development, and improves consistency in delivery.
Contrast: Project manager — a PMO provides organizational governance and support across multiple initiatives, while a project manager leads the work of a specific project.
Example: A company PMO issues common schedule and risk templates, reviews monthly status reports, and helps project managers escalate decisions through governance channels.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide) Seventh Edition, Project management office, page 239

Core Meaning: The document that describes how the project will be executed, monitored and controlled, and closed.
Expansion: The project management plan integrates the subsidiary plans and baselines that guide how the project will be managed. It is the central reference for execution, monitoring, control, and closure rather than a simple schedule or task list. PMI treats it as the primary management document that brings scope, schedule, cost, risk, quality, resources, communications, and other management approaches into one coherent whole.
Deep Dive:
What it is: The project management plan is the master management document for the project. It consolidates approved baselines and subsidiary plans so the team has a clear, coordinated way to run the work.

Why it matters and where it applies: it matters because projects fail when teams manage scope, schedule, cost, risk, and governance in isolation. The plan creates integration across these areas and is used in predictive, hybrid, and adaptive environments, though the level of detail and formality may vary.

The most common misconception: that the project management plan is just a timeline. In practice, the schedule is only one component; the full plan also defines decision rules, change control, reporting, resource approaches, and how performance will be managed.

How it shows up in real projects: a project manager uses the plan to align the schedule baseline, risk approach, communication rules, and change process so the team and stakeholders know how the project will operate from start to finish.
Contrast: Project schedule — the project management plan is the integrated guide for managing the whole project; the schedule is one component focused on timing and sequencing.
Example: For a factory upgrade, the team approves a project management plan that combines the scope baseline, schedule baseline, cost baseline, risk management approach, and change control process.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge PMBOK Guide Seventh Edition and Process Groups A Practice Guide — project management plan, page 196

Core Meaning: The extent to which a project is achieving its intended measures of progress, outcomes, and delivery effectiveness.
Expansion: Project performance is the assessed condition of a project against planned or agreed criteria such as scope, schedule, cost, quality, risk, and value delivery. It is understood through the interpretation of performance data, performance information, and performance reports rather than through raw figures alone. In PMI practice, the concept supports decision making, forecasting, and corrective or preventive action.
Contrast: Work performance data — project performance is the overall interpreted state of the project, while work performance data is the raw observation and measurement used to assess it.
Example: A project team reviews schedule variance, defect trends, budget consumption, and stakeholder acceptance to determine whether overall project performance is improving or deteriorating.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: The overall effort of defining scope, objectives, and the course of action needed to achieve project goals.
Expansion: Project planning develops the project management plan and its subsidiary plans and baselines, defining how the project will be executed, monitored, and closed. Good planning is iterative and progressively elaborated as more information becomes available.
Contrast: Planning Process Group - planning as a concept is the broad activity, while the Planning Process Group is the formal grouping of planning processes.
Example: During planning, a team defines scope, builds a WBS, sequences activities, estimates costs, and sets baselines.
Source: Concept aligned with the PMBOK Guide Planning Process Group

Core Meaning: A structured examination of project status, deliverables, or readiness used to support governance decisions and improvement actions.
Expansion: A project review is a formal check on some aspect of project progress, quality, risk, or readiness at a defined point in time. It may be used to confirm continued alignment, approve movement into the next phase, or identify corrective action that is needed. In PMI practice, reviews are an important mechanism for governance, transparency, and organizational learning.
Contrast: Project audit — a project review is typically a management or governance examination of status or readiness, while a project audit is a more formal and often independent assessment of compliance, controls, or performance.
Example: At the end of design, a steering committee conducts a project review to assess scope completeness, unresolved risks, and readiness to authorize build work.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A governance body of senior stakeholders that provides strategic direction, oversight, and decision support for a project.
Expansion: A project steering committee is typically composed of sponsors, executives, and other senior stakeholders who guide the project at a governance level rather than manage day-to-day delivery. It reviews major risks, escalated issues, funding, priorities, and alignment with organisational strategy, and it helps resolve decisions beyond the project manager's authority. In practice it is most common on large, complex, politically sensitive, or high-investment initiatives where executive oversight materially affects success.
Contrast: Project team — the project team delivers the work; the steering committee provides strategic oversight, escalation support, and governance decisions.
Example: For a hospital construction project, the steering committee meets monthly to review milestone status, approve major scope trade-offs, and resolve conflicts between clinical operations and construction timelines.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A concise description of the desired future state or intended outcome that the project seeks to realise.
Expansion: A Project Vision Statement expresses the purpose and aspirational direction of the project in clear, outcome-focused language. It is used to align stakeholders, guide decision-making, and keep delivery focused on the value the project is meant to create. In PMI usage it appears more as a practitioner concept than as a formal core glossary term, especially in agile and product-oriented contexts.
Contrast: Project Objective — a vision statement describes the desired future state and overarching intent; an objective states a specific result to be achieved, often with measurable criteria.
Example: In a citizen-services transformation, the vision statement is to create a single digital entry point where residents can complete the most common services in minutes rather than across multiple offices and forms.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: The systematic control, storage, retention, retrieval, and disposition of project and organisational records.
Expansion: Records management is the disciplined handling of records so that project information remains accurate, accessible, protected, and auditable over time. In project settings it supports compliance, traceability, knowledge retention, and defensible decision histories. It commonly covers items such as approvals, contracts, change records, correspondence, and lessons learned.
Contrast: Configuration management — records management governs retention and control of records broadly, while configuration management focuses on the integrity and control of deliverable and product-related baselines and changes.
Example: On an infrastructure project, signed permits, inspection logs, approved change orders, and vendor correspondence are retained under a controlled filing and retention process to satisfy audit and legal obligations.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A document used to capture and maintain a defined set of project information that requires ongoing tracking and updating.
Expansion: In PMI usage, a register is a structured project record for a specific category of information, such as stakeholders, risks, or assumptions. It is maintained over time rather than issued once. Its value is that it provides a current reference for ownership, status, and follow-up as the project evolves.
Contrast: Report — a register is a living tracking record for a defined class of items; a report communicates status, analysis, or results to an audience.
Example: A project team updates its stakeholder register whenever new stakeholders are identified or existing stakeholders change in influence or interest.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Seventh Edition, Project artifacts section, p. 153

Core Meaning: A formally documented request to modify a project document, deliverable, or baseline.
Expansion: A requested change is a proposal to alter scope, schedule, cost, or other project elements, submitted for evaluation through integrated change control. It must be assessed and approved or rejected before the change is implemented.
Contrast: Distinct from an approved change, which has passed through change control, a requested change is still awaiting a decision.
Example: A stakeholder submits a requested change to add a reporting feature, which the change control board then reviews.
Source: PMBOK Guide

Core Meaning: A formal acknowledgement or approval that a deliverable, decision, phase result, or document has been reviewed and accepted by an authorised party.
Expansion: Sign-off is the act of recording acceptance or approval at a defined point in the project. It is commonly used for deliverables, requirements, baselines, phase gates, and closures to show that the right stakeholder has reviewed and accepted the result. In practice, sign-off supports governance, traceability, and accountability rather than replacing quality verification or stakeholder engagement.
Contrast: Validate Scope — Sign-off is the formal approval record, while Validate Scope is the process of formalising acceptance of completed deliverables.
Example: At the end of user acceptance testing, the business sponsor signs off on the release package to confirm the system meets agreed acceptance criteria before deployment.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A defined segment of a project, often ending in a review or decision point.
Expansion: A stage groups related work between governance checkpoints, allowing progress to be assessed before committing to the next part. Stages are commonly separated by gates where continuation is approved, changed, or stopped.
Contrast: Gate — a stage is the period of work; a gate is the decision checkpoint at its boundary.
Example: The project advances from the design stage to the build stage after passing a stage gate.
Source: Not a formal PMI glossary entry; defined for glossary completeness

Core Meaning: A formal decision point at the end of a stage where the project is reviewed and a go, no-go, or change decision is made.
Expansion: A stage gate is a control point used to assess whether a project should proceed to the next stage. It is associated with phase-based governance, business review, and oversight of investment decisions. PMI uses the term for structured review points where deliverables, risks, readiness, and business alignment are evaluated before authorising continuation.
Contrast: Phase gate — stage gate and phase gate are often used similarly, but stage gate emphasises the governance decision point at the end of a stage.
Example: After concept development for a new product, executives review cost, risk, and market fit at a stage gate before approving detailed design.
Source: PMI Lexicon of Project Management Terms — Stage gate

Core Meaning: An advisory body of senior stakeholders who provide guidance, oversight, and decision support for a project, program, or portfolio.
Expansion: A steering committee is a governance body made up of senior stakeholders with authority or influence over the work. It provides direction, resolves escalated issues, and helps keep the initiative aligned with organizational priorities. PMI uses the term for groups that support decision making beyond the authority of the delivery team. It is especially common in complex, cross-functional, or high-visibility initiatives.
Contrast: Project team — the steering committee governs and guides the work, while the project team performs and manages the day to day delivery.
Example: A bank's digital transformation project uses a steering committee of business, technology, and compliance executives to approve major scope decisions and resolve conflicts across departments.
Source: PMI Lexicon of Project Management Terms, steering committee, page 27

Core Meaning: A component of the project management plan that describes how a specific aspect of the project will be planned, managed, monitored, and controlled.
Expansion: A subsidiary plan is one of the supporting plans that together make up the project management plan. Each subsidiary plan addresses a particular management domain such as scope, schedule, cost, quality, resources, communications, risk, procurement, or stakeholder engagement. PMI uses the concept to show that integrated project management is built from coordinated domain-specific planning rather than from a single undifferentiated plan. The term is foundational because it explains how planning detail is structured and governed across the project.
Deep Dive:
What it is: A subsidiary plan is a formal part of the project management plan devoted to one management area. It sets the rules, methods, thresholds, and responsibilities for that area so the team can manage work consistently.

Why it matters and where it applies: projects quickly become difficult to control if planning exists only at a high level. Subsidiary plans create usable management guidance for delivery teams and are especially important on projects with multiple workstreams, compliance needs, external suppliers, or significant risk exposure.

The most common misconception: that a subsidiary plan is just a detailed schedule or an optional attachment. In PMI usage, it is broader than scheduling and is not merely supplemental notes; it is an approved planning component that supports integrated management.

How it shows up in real projects: a project may include a risk management plan defining probability-impact scales, a communications management plan defining reporting cadence, and a scope management plan defining how changes will be evaluated, all under the umbrella of the project management plan.
Contrast: Project management plan — a subsidiary plan is one component plan for a specific domain; the project management plan is the integrated, approved whole.
Example: On an ERP implementation, the project manager maintains a stakeholder engagement plan, risk management plan, and quality management plan as separate subsidiary plans within the overall project management plan.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide) Seventh Edition, page 239

Core Meaning: Knowledge that is personal, experience-based, and difficult to fully articulate or document.
Expansion: Tacit knowledge consists of know-how, judgment, intuition, and practical insight developed through experience. PMI distinguishes it from explicit knowledge, which can be more easily codified in documents, repositories, and procedures. In project work, tacit knowledge is often transferred through observation, mentoring, collaboration, and shared practice rather than through formal records alone.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Tacit knowledge is the unwritten expertise people carry in their heads, shaped by experience, pattern recognition, and context. It includes practical know-how such as how to navigate a stakeholder, spot early warning signs, or sequence work effectively in a specific environment.

Why it matters and where it applies: many critical project decisions rely on judgment that is not fully captured in templates or lessons-learned files. It matters in estimating, risk identification, stakeholder engagement, technical troubleshooting, and onboarding, especially where local context or specialist expertise strongly affects outcomes.

The most common misconception: that documenting lessons learned fully captures what experts know. Documentation helps, but much high-value knowledge remains embedded in behavior, relationships, and situational judgment that must be shared through interaction.

How it shows up in real projects: a veteran construction manager may know which subcontractor warning signs predict delay, or an experienced product owner may sense when stakeholder resistance will surface despite positive status reports. Teams surface tacit knowledge through peer reviews, shadowing, communities of practice, and retrospective discussion.
Contrast: Explicit knowledge — tacit knowledge is hard to codify and transfer directly; explicit knowledge can be documented, stored, and retrieved more easily.
Example: An experienced scheduler adjusts a dependency plan based on past vendor behavior and local permitting realities that are not captured in the formal schedule guidance.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Sixth Edition, page 181

Core Meaning: The deliberate adaptation of a project management approach, governance, methods, and artifacts to fit the context of a specific project.
Expansion: Tailoring is the process of selecting and adjusting project practices so they are appropriate for the work, environment, stakeholders, and level of uncertainty involved. PMI treats it as a core responsibility of effective project delivery rather than an exception to a standard method. It helps teams avoid both under-governing complex work and overburdening simple work with unnecessary process.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Tailoring is the intentional fit-for-purpose design of how a project will be managed. It includes adapting life cycle choices, governance controls, reporting, planning detail, and delivery practices to the needs of the initiative.

Why it matters and where it applies: no single approach works equally well for every project. Tailoring matters in all domains because project size, risk, complexity, regulatory pressure, team capability, and delivery cadence differ across efforts.

The most common misconception: that tailoring means skipping discipline or ignoring standards. In practice, it means applying discipline thoughtfully so that project management effort supports outcomes instead of becoming mechanical overhead.

How it shows up in real projects: a regulated construction project may require formal stage gates and heavy documentation, while a digital product effort may use rolling-wave planning, lightweight decision logs, and iterative delivery within the same organizational framework.
Contrast: Customization — tailoring adapts the project management approach for context; customization more broadly changes features or components of a product, service, or method.
Example: A company uses its standard governance model for all projects, but for a six-week internal automation effort it reduces approval layers, uses a simplified risk register, and replaces monthly status packs with a live dashboard.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge, Seventh Edition, section 3 Model, Methods, and Artifacts and section 4 Tailoring

Core Meaning: Comparing actual technical achievement against planned technical measures to detect deviations.
Expansion: Technical performance analysis (or measurement) compares delivered technical metrics, such as weight, capacity, or response time, against the values planned during planning. Significant variances signal scope or quality risks and may indicate the project will not meet requirements. It is used in risk monitoring and quality control to catch problems early.
Contrast: Earned value analysis - earned value tracks cost and schedule performance, whereas technical performance analysis tracks achievement of technical requirements.
Example: Measuring that a prototype drone weighs 1.4 kg against a 1.2 kg target, flagging a design risk.
Source: PMBOK Guide - technical performance analysis (data analysis)

Core Meaning: Transfer of responsibility is the point at which ownership, custody, or accountability for a deliverable shifts from one party to another, such as from seller to buyer or from project team to operations.
Expansion: In project management practice, this concept marks the formal handoff of a product, asset, service, or obligation between parties. It matters because risks, acceptance conditions, support expectations, and liability often change at that moment. Teams usually make the transfer explicit through acceptance records, transition plans, commissioning steps, or operational handover criteria.
Contrast: Transition — transfer of responsibility is the specific shift in accountability or custody, while transition is the broader process of moving work or outcomes into use or operations.
Example: After final acceptance testing, a project team hands a new warehouse management system to the operations support group, which assumes incident response and maintenance responsibility.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: The movement of a project output, capability, or result from delivery into operational use, ownership, or adoption.
Expansion: Transition refers to the activities that move a project deliverable from the project environment into business operations, customer use, or another receiving organisation. It often includes handoff, training, readiness checks, support arrangements, and confirmation that the receiving party can sustain the result. In PMI practice, transition matters because delivering the output is not the same as realising value from it.
Contrast: Closing — transition focuses on handoff and operational adoption of deliverables, while closing finalises the project or phase administratively and contractually.
Example: After building a new customer portal, the project team transitions it to the operations team through training, support documentation, cutover planning, and service monitoring.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A transition plan describes how project outputs, deliverables, or capabilities will be transferred into operational use and sustained after project or phase completion.
Expansion: Within PMI practice, a transition plan addresses the activities, responsibilities, timing, readiness checks, and support arrangements needed to move from delivery into operations, ownership, or benefits realisation. It often covers training, handoff, support, documentation, acceptance, and operational preparedness. The plan reduces disruption by clarifying how the receiving organisation will assume control and continue the work product effectively.
Contrast: Project management plan — the project management plan governs how the project is managed overall; the transition plan focuses specifically on operational handoff and adoption.
Example: After implementing a new enterprise resource planning system, the project team uses a transition plan to schedule user training, service-desk support, data handoff, acceptance sign-off, and transfer to the operations team.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A project delivery support structure that focuses on coaching teams, building agile skills and capabilities, and mentoring sponsors and product owners.
Expansion: PMI defines the Value Delivery Office as a project delivery support structure that focuses on coaching teams, building agile skills and capabilities throughout the organization, and mentoring sponsors and product owners to be more effective in those roles. It supports value delivery by strengthening delivery practices and decision quality rather than only enforcing governance. The concept reflects an evolution from control-oriented support models toward capability-building and outcome focus.
Contrast: PMO — a VDO is specifically oriented to value delivery and agile capability building, while a PMO is a broader project management support structure.
Example: An enterprise creates a VDO to coach product owners, improve agile practices across teams, and help sponsors make faster value-based decisions.
Source: The PMI Lexicon of Project Management Terms — Value Delivery Office (VDO), page 106

Core Meaning: An actual condition that differs from the expected condition in the baseline plan.
Expansion: Variation is any difference between what was planned or expected and what actually occurs, whether in cost, schedule, scope, or a process measurement. In quality management, variation is analyzed to distinguish normal common-cause noise from special-cause problems. Understanding variation helps decide whether corrective action is needed.
Contrast: Special Cause - special cause is one assignable source of variation, whereas variation is the broader difference from expected conditions.
Example: A task planned for five days that actually takes seven shows a schedule variation.
Source: PMBOK Guide - quality / variation

Core Meaning: A practice for managing and tracking changes to documents, code, designs, or other project deliverables over time.
Expansion: Version control preserves the history of changes, identifies which revision is current, and supports coordination when multiple people update the same work product. In project work it reduces confusion, rework, and accidental loss of approved content. It is especially important where deliverables evolve rapidly or many contributors are involved.
Contrast: Configuration management — version control tracks revisions to items, while configuration management governs the broader identification, control, status, and integrity of configuration items.
Example: A product team stores requirements, interface designs, and source code in a repository so it can trace each approved revision and roll back if a change creates defects.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A concise description of the desired future end state a project or product aims to achieve.
Expansion: A vision articulates the aspirational outcome and purpose that gives a project or product direction and aligns stakeholders. In agile, a product vision keeps the team focused on the bigger goal behind the backlog and informs prioritization. A clear vision lets people make consistent decisions without constant direction.
Contrast: Objective - an objective is a specific measurable target, whereas a vision is the broader aspirational end state that objectives serve.
Example: The statement 'make local public transport so easy that residents prefer it to driving' framing a transit app project.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A formal approval to begin work on a project, phase, or activity.
Expansion: Work authorization is the approval that permits work to start in line with project governance and planning controls. It helps ensure that execution begins only when prerequisites, approvals, and timing conditions have been satisfied. In practice it is used to prevent premature or unauthorized work and to align release of effort with approved plans.
Contrast: Work authorization system — work authorization is the approval to proceed; the system is the method used to issue and control that approval.
Example: A construction manager receives written authorization to start foundation work only after design review and permit approval are complete.
Source: PMI Lexicon of Project Management Terms, work authorization

Core Meaning: A collection of formal, documented procedures that defines how project work is authorized so it is performed at the right time and in the proper sequence.
Expansion: A work authorization system is the governance mechanism used to release work in a controlled way. It links approvals, sequencing, and organizational procedures so teams do not begin work before the required conditions are met. PMI treats it as an important control for coordinating execution across phases, teams, and dependencies.
Deep Dive:
What it is: A work authorization system is the formal process or set of procedures used to approve work before it begins. It may include written approvals, electronic workflows, release gates, or other documented controls that govern when work can start.

Why it matters and where it applies: It matters because projects often involve interdependent work, limited resources, compliance obligations, and multiple decision makers. It is especially useful in predictive and hybrid environments, and in regulated or capital-intensive projects where timing and sequence must be tightly controlled.

The most common misconception: that it is just a signature or a one-time approval. In practice it is a broader control system that can govern the release of work throughout the project life cycle, not merely the initial start of the project.

How it shows up in real projects: A sponsor approves a phase, the PMO issues a formal release, procurement confirms prerequisites, and the team starts only after the required authorizations are in place. The system creates traceability for who approved the work, when it was released, and under what conditions.
Contrast: Work authorization — work authorization is the approval itself; the work authorization system is the formal process that issues and controls that approval.
Example: Before a vendor begins installation, the project office requires a signed release form, completed safety checks, and phase approval through the organisation's authorization workflow.
Source: PMI Lexicon of Project Management Terms, work authorization system

Core Meaning: The raw observations and measurements identified during activities performed to carry out the project work.
Expansion: Work performance data is unprocessed factual project data captured while work is being performed. PMI treats it as the basic input for monitoring and controlling, before analysis or interpretation has occurred. Typical examples include actual dates, actual costs, defect counts, and completed work quantities.
Contrast: Work performance information — work performance data is raw observation and measurement, while work performance information is that data after analysis and contextualisation.
Example: A project team records actual labour hours, number of test failures, and percentage of foundation concrete poured during the reporting period.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge, Sixth Edition, work performance data, page 143

Core Meaning: Performance data collected from various controlling processes, analysed in context and integrated based on relationships across areas.
Expansion: Work performance information is project performance data that has been processed so it can support interpretation and decision making. PMI distinguishes it from raw data because it has been analysed, combined, or compared against baselines, thresholds, or expectations. It is commonly used to identify trends, variances, and emerging issues that need management attention.
Contrast: Work performance reports — work performance information is analysed performance insight, while work performance reports are the formal physical or electronic representations used to present that information.
Example: After comparing actual progress and spending against the baseline, the project manager identifies that the project is two weeks late on critical path work but still within cost tolerance.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge, Sixth Edition, work performance information, page 144
